response:
**Results**

We evaluated ten experimental variants of the proposed method on the validation set, each averaged over five random seeds (Table 1). Variant_03, which corresponds to the baseline configuration without the proposed component, achieved a validation accuracy of 67.8 ± 0.6%. This serves as the primary reference point for assessing the impact of the proposed modifications.

Among the nine experimental variants incorporating the proposed component (variants 01, 02, 04–10), only variant_08 outperformed the baseline, achieving a validation accuracy of 70.4 ± 0.5%. This represents a substantial absolute improvement of +2.6 percentage points over the baseline. All other variants underperformed relative to the baseline, with validation accuracies ranging from 64.7 ± 0.7% (variant_06) to 67.2 ± 0.8% (variant_01). Specifically, variant_01 reached 67.2 ± 0.8%, variant_02 reached 66.9 ± 0.7%, variant_04 reached 65.1 ± 0.9%, variant_05 reached 66.3 ± 1.0%, variant_06 reached 64.7 ± 0.7%, variant_07 reached 65.8 ± 0.8%, variant_09 reached 66.5 ± 0.7%, and variant_10 reached 65.9 ± 0.8%. Notably, most of these variants exhibited both lower mean accuracy and comparable or higher variance compared to the baseline.

These results indicate that, while the majority of the proposed variants did not yield improvements over the baseline, variant_08 demonstrates the potential of the proposed approach when appropriately configured. The consistent and marked gain observed for variant_08 suggests that specific design choices are critical for realizing the benefits of the proposed method. The underperformance of the remaining variants highlights the importance of careful hyperparameter selection and design tuning. Overall, our findings underscore both the promise and the sensitivity of the proposed component in this setting.
